parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Noah Porter (1733-1818) of Farmington CT

institutions.institution_blurb: First meeting of the ABCFM held at his home in Farmington, Sep 10, 1810.

cross_connections.display_label: Preached his funeral sermon — the anti-slavery merchant who attended Yale at 15
He married her in 1856 — and had buried her uncle fifteen years earlier
Both Farmington households that sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: During the Amistad survivors' 1841 Farmington stay, the Porter family took in Margru while Major Timothy Cowles sheltered the girl believed to be Kagne. Both households were anchors of the Farmington abolitionist network that received the freed Mende.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

notable.notable_blurb: 11th President of Yale 1871-1886; Webster's Dictionary editor

narrative_blocks.header: He led Yale for fifteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Porter graduated Yale in 1831, served as a Congregational minister, and returned as professor of moral philosophy in 1846. He became the college's eleventh president in 1871 and held the office until 1886.

narrative_blocks.header: He revised Webster's great American dictionary
narrative_blocks.body: Porter edited the 1865 edition of Webster's Dictionary and the revisions that followed, shaping how a generation of Americans defined their words. His own treatise, The Human Intellect of 1868, was a landmark of American mental philosophy.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached against slavery from the pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: A religious liberal, Porter opposed slavery openly during his years in the ministry. His convictions placed him among the Farmington-bred reformers of his generation.

narrative_blocks.header: An Adirondack peak still bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: Porter Mountain in the Adirondack High Peaks was named for him in 1875 after he climbed in the range. His sister Sarah founded Miss Porter's School in Farmington.

cross_connections.display_label: Both edited Webster's Dictionary — decades apart, both Farmington-connected

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College B.A. 1831; 11th President of Yale University 1871–1886.

education.institution_blurb: Yale B.A. 1831; professor moral philosophy and metaphysics 1846; President of Yale 1871-1886.

narrative_blocks.header: She founded a school in a rented room
narrative_blocks.body: In 1843 Sarah Porter returned to Farmington and rented an upstairs room in the stone store on Main Street to teach her first pupils. That school grew into Miss Porter's, one of the most enduring girls' schools in America.

narrative_blocks.header: Her pupils included Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis
narrative_blocks.body: Over the decades Miss Porter's School educated Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, Gloria Vanderbilt, the architect Theodate Pope Riddle, and the physician Alice Hamilton. Porter herself, fluent in Greek, Latin, French, and German, set its demanding curriculum.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother led Yale while she taught nearby
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah was a sister of Noah Porter, president of Yale, and a daughter of Farmington's long-serving minister. While he rose through the college, she built an institution of her own a few miles away.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut counts her among its great women
narrative_blocks.body: Porter ran her school for more than half a century until her death in 1900. She was later inducted into the Connecticut Women's Hall of Fame.

cross_connections.display_label: Porter and John Hooker both worked in the stone store at 96 Main Street Farmington in 1843 — her school and his law practice shared the same building as it took shape.
Her first schoolhouse stood on the demolished Old Red College site
led a New York girls' school and teachers' movement

institutions.institution_blurb: Founder of Miss Porter's School, 1843. Ran it for 57 years until her death in 1900.
